Mongolia Ambassador in Ladakh, as Buddha relics are taken to the union territory. Mongolia, landlocked between Russia & China, sees India as its 3rd & spiritual neighbour due to Buddhism connect.

International Buddhist Confederation (IBC) (@IbcWorldOrg)

On the sidelines of the Holy Relics Exposition to Ladakh, the International Buddhist Confederation welcomed dignitaries at the exhibition on Guru Padmasambhava in Bhoti language inaugurated by H.E. Mr. @GanboldDambajav, Ambassador of #Mongolia to India at the Dharma Centre, in #Leh

Ven. Dorje Stanzin, President, Ladakh Gonpa Association delivered the welcome address, followed by Shartse Khensur Rinpoche Jangchup Choeden, Secretary General, IBC and Shri Sanjit Rodrigues, Secretary Culture and Tourism in UT Ladakh.
#BuddhaRelicsInLadakh

tweets.title:
R to @NetramDefence: Claim 3: The Cost of Alternative Routes

Facts:
The "not much at all" cost claim is mathematically false. Bypassing Malacca requires using the Lombok or Sunda straits.  

Distance: Re-routing through Lombok adds ~1,200 nautical miles and 3–5 extra days at sea.

Depth: The Sunda Strait is too shallow (~20m) for fully-laden VLCCs (Supertankers), forcing the world's energy supply onto the longer Lombok route.

Economics: For global shipping, a 5-day delay per vessel translates to millions in fuel, insurance spikes, and supply chain "bullwhip effects" that a global economy cannot easily absorb.

tweets.title:
R to @NetramDefence: Claim 2: Shipping Traffic Proximity

Facts:
The claim that ships stay 50km away from Indian islands ignores maritime geography. The Six Degree Channel (the Grand Channel) is the primary gateway for all East-West trade entering the Malacca Strait. Every vessel coming from the Suez Canal or the Persian Gulf converges at this specific latitude. Ships don't need to "hug the coast" for a terminal like the Galathea Bay ICTT to be effective; they simply have no other deep-water entry point into the Malacca funnel without a massive detour.

tweets.title:
Certain social media accounts are questioning the importance of the Great Nicobar Project with illogical arguments here is the fact check of their claims.

Claim 1: The "Width" Comparison (50km vs. 200km)

Facts:
Comparing the Strait of Hormuz to Great Nicobar based solely on physical width is a 19th-century view of naval warfare. In 2026, a "choke point" is defined by the range of stationed sensors and strike systems. With BrahMos missiles (range 450km–800km) and P-8I Poseidon aircraft operating from the islands, India can monitor and interdict the entire 200km Six Degree Channel with 100% overlap. Physical narrowness is irrelevant when the entire gap is within high-precision strike range.

BREAKING: China to Offer Zero Tariffs to All African Partners

Starting May 1, China will extend zero-tariff treatment to all African countries with diplomatic ties, marking a major shift in China–Africa trade relations.

What it means:
- Duty-free access for African exports into China
- Push to boost trade, investment, and supply chains
- Greater economic alignment across the Global South

Beijing says it aims to import more African goods and expand growth opportunities, calling it a landmark step in international economic cooperation.

MYANMAR: MILITARY REGAINS MOMENTUM IN CIVIL WAR

Myanmar’s military, the Tatmadaw, appears set to resume major offensives after regaining ground in the ongoing civil war.

Key developments:
- Reinforced by tens of thousands of new conscripts
- Opposition weakened by infighting, supply issues, and factional splits
- Some rebel groups have reduced or exited active combat

Despite this shift, analysts say the conflict is far from over, with resistance expected to continue until a political settlement is reached.
